vitals.field_size: 4,000+ participants across all formats (2025); 350-team cap in 8-hour team classes (2026)
vitals.start_format: Rolling start behind a pace motorcycle; solo or relay teams exchanging a timing transponder in the pits
vitals.feed_zones: Pit-based; teams self-manage support from the pit lane
vitals.cutoff_time: Timed formats: 4-hour and 8-hour enduros; Attack 240 has an 8-hour limit

route_options.name: 8-hour enduro (solo/team)
route_options.notes: Most laps in 8 hours on the 5.807 km circuit; 2025 leader logged 58 laps (~336.8 km)

route_options.name: 4-hour enduro
route_options.notes: Most laps in 4 hours; includes road, women, flat-bar/recumbent, city-bike and family classes

route_options.name: Attack 240
route_options.notes: Fixed 40 laps x 5.807 km = 232.28 km within an 8-hour limit

climate.description: Mid-November in Mie Prefecture: cool autumn conditions, a far cry from the summer heat associated with the circuit's famous motorcycle race.
challenges: Wind exposure on the West Straight over a long timed effort

terrain.surface: Fully paved racing circuit (10-16 m wide)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 69. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 67. FLAG (2026-07-24 editorial wave 5): prestige=5, cultural_impact=5, field_depth=4, organization=5, scenic_experience=4 and descent_technicality=4 were scored against the Suzuka 8 Hours MOTORCYCLE race this profile previously described. The actual event (Suzuka 8 Hours Enduro, amateur bicycle circuit enduro) has a materially different honest scoring basis — recommend a human re-score; numbers left untouched per no-silent-reshuffle rule.

course_description.character: Laps of the Suzuka Circuit International Racing Course — the 5.807 km figure-eight famous from Formula 1 — ridden counterclockwise, the opposite direction to motorsport. This is a timed circuit enduro, not a point-to-point course: solo riders or relay teams accumulate as many laps as possible in 4 or 8 hours, or ride the fixed 40-lap Attack 240 (232.28 km). The lap rolls constantly through the chicane, West Straight, Spoon, the hairpin, the 25R-Degner complex and the descending S-curves — technical circuit features rather than named climbs — on fully paved racing tarmac 10 to 16 metres wide. Total distance and climbing depend entirely on how many laps you complete; the 2025 8-hour leader logged 58 laps, roughly 336.8 km.
course_description.signature_challenge: Pacing a 4- or 8-hour circuit effort: the lap's rhythm — descending S-curves, the Degner complex, the hairpin, and the rising drag to the line — repeats every 5.807 km, and lap consistency is the whole game.
suffering_zones: The closing hours of the 8-hour enduro: most-laps scoring means there is never a point where slowing down doesn't cost you.
The rise to the finish line each lap — the official course guidance notes the drag up to the line, and the official 2019 report describes a headwind on the home straight.

logistics.airport: Chubu Centrair International (NGO), ~1.5h; Osaka Kansai (KIX) also workable
logistics.lodging_strategy: On-site hotel at Suzuka Circuit, nearby hotels in Suzuka city, or camping areas.
logistics.transport: Suzuka Circuit Ino station (Ise Railway) plus shuttle/taxi; the venue is a full resort complex with parking.
logistics.food: Circuit resort concessions on site; teams typically self-cater in the pits.

history.origin_story: Began in 2000 as one of Japan's early bicycle circuit enduros; the 8-hour race was added at the third edition. It has grown into one of the country's biggest participation cycling festivals, drawing over 4,000 riders across its formats. It borrows the '8 Hours' name and venue from the famous Suzuka 8 Hours motorcycle race (held since 1978) but is an entirely separate amateur bicycle event, run in November and ridden in the reverse direction.
notable_moments: 2025 (26th edition, November 1): 4,000+ participants across the 4-hour enduro, 8-hour enduro and Attack 240
2026 (27th edition): scheduled November 14, registration open July 17 - October 5
history.reputation: A high-volume Japanese amateur enduro festival on a world-famous circuit — held since 2000, little covered in Western cycling media, and routinely confused online with the Suzuka 8 Hours motorcycle race.

biased_opinion.verdict: Real Bike Event, Wrong Name — and Not a Gran Fondo
biased_opinion.summary: Housekeeping first: 'Suzuka Endurance Road' is not a thing. No event uses that name — it's a hybrid of the Suzuka 8 Hours Enduro (a real amateur bicycle event) and the Suzuka 8 Hours Endurance Road Race (the famous FIM motorcycle race). This profile previously carried the motorcycle race's dates, videos and atmosphere; all of that has been purged. What's left is genuinely worth knowing about: in November 2025 more than 4,000 cyclists took over the Formula 1 circuit for timed enduros — 4 hours, 8 hours solo or in relay teams of up to six, or the fixed 40-lap Attack 240 — riding the 5.807 km lap counterclockwise on closed racing tarmac, with transponder timing, pit lanes and rolling starts. But be honest about the format: this is lap racing against a clock, not a gran fondo. There is no course to discover, no summit, no scenery beyond the circuit complex — the appeal is the circuit itself and the event around it, and registration, rules and communications are in Japanese.
strengths: Riding a genuinely world-famous F1 circuit — S-curves, Spoon, the West Straight — closed, fully paved and 10-16 m wide
Big event with real infrastructure: 4,000+ participants in 2025, transponder relay timing, pit lanes, on-site resort hotel and camping
Formats for everyone: 8-hour solo or 2-6 rider relay, 4-hour classes including city-bike and family, and the fixed-distance Attack 240
Long-running: held since 2000, with the 8-hour format added at the third edition — the 27th edition runs in 2026
weaknesses: Not a gran fondo in any sense — it's a timed circuit enduro/relay; there is no point-to-point course, and total distance depends on laps ridden
Zero route variety: the same 5.807 km lap for up to eight hours, inside a motorsports complex
A domestic-facing event: registration, rules and communications are in Japanese, with little English-language support
The event's own '8 Hours' branding invites confusion with the motorcycle race that shares its name — this database included; the previous version of this profile described the wrong sport
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you're in Japan in November and want to ride the actual Suzuka F1 circuit hard for four or eight hours, this is a well-established way to do it — provided you can handle Japanese-language registration. Just don't come looking for a gran fondo, and don't confuse it with the motorcycle race that shares its name.

final_verdict.one_liner: Japan's big amateur circuit enduro on the Suzuka F1 track — a timed lap race, not a gran fondo (and not the motorcycle race).
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if a timed 4/8-hour team or solo enduro on a legendary closed circuit appeals and you can navigate Japanese-language registration. No if you want a point-to-point gran fondo with real terrain — this is the same 5.807 km lap all day.
final_verdict.alternatives: Shimano Suzuka Road (September, same circuit) for a mass-start road-race festival format; Japan's public-road fondos (e.g. Niseko Classic) for actual open-road racing.

youtube_data.search_query: Suzuka Endurance Road gran fondo cycling Suzuka Circuit
youtube_data.purge_note: 2026-07-24 editorial wave 5: all previous videos, quotes and rider_intel were FIM EWC / Suzuka 8 Hours MOTORCYCLE race or vintage motorsports content — cross-sport mislabeling — and were removed. No bicycle-event footage was verified in their place.

eligibility.notes: IDENTITY CORRECTION (2026-07-24 editorial wave 5): this profile was a conflation of two different sports. 'Suzuka Endurance Road' matched no real event; the vitals (July 3-5 2026 date, 'hundreds of teams', pit-stop feed zones) and all youtube_data/rider_intel described the 47th FIM Suzuka 8 Hours MOTORCYCLE endurance race (suzukacircuit.jp/eng/8tai/), and the prior eligibility source was the motorcycle race's Wikipedia page. The real bicycle event at this venue is the Suzuka 8 Hours Enduro (suzuka8h.powertag.jp): 27th edition November 14, 2026, registration open July 17-October 5; 26th edition held November 1, 2025 with 4,000+ participants. Renamed name/display_name to 'Suzuka 8 Hours Enduro' (slug unchanged to preserve URLs, per flandrien-ride precedent); vitals rebuilt around the bicycle event: date Nov 14 2026, vitals.distance set to the Attack 240 fixed format (232.28 km, the only fixed-distance class, per race-through-poland nominal-distance precedent); the 4h/8h timed enduros are lap-dependent with no fixed distance; elevation left null (no official cumulative figure), motorcycle media purged. Distinct from Shimano Suzuka Road (September 26-27, 2026, same circuit). Verified via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research + curl of suzuka8h.powertag.jp.

catalog_flags.status_note: fondo_rating.discipline is 'gran_fondo' but this is a timed bicycle circuit enduro / team relay (4h/8h most-laps formats plus fixed 232.28 km Attack 240) on a closed motorsports circuit — doesn't cleanly fit the gran_fondo taxonomy or its scoring dimensions (distance/climbing assume a fixed course; prestige/cultural_impact were previously scored against the Suzuka 8 Hours MOTORCYCLE race this profile conflated). Recommend a human call on reclassifying discipline, re-scoring, or removing the profile, consistent with how flandrien-ride.json flags taxonomy mismatches rather than silently changing the discipline field.
